questions.question: Explain the difference between recursive and iterative DNS queries.
keyPoints: Recursive query: DNS server performs complete resolution and returns final answer to client
Iterative query: DNS server returns best answer or referral to another server
Client or server may need to make multiple queries in iterative approach
Recursive is more client-friendly but places load on single server

questions.question: List and explain the function of at least four different DNS record types.
keyPoints: A Record: Maps domain to IPv4 address
AAAA Record: Maps domain to IPv6 address
CNAME: Creates alias pointing to canonical name
MX Record: Specifies mail server for domain
NS Record: Delegates authority to name server
PTR Record: Reverse DNS lookup mapping

questions.question: Describe the step-by-step process of DNS resolution when a user types 'www.example.com' in a browser.
keyPoints: Client queries local DNS resolver
Resolver checks cache; if miss, queries root server
Root server directs to .com TLD server
TLD server directs to authoritative server for example.com
Authoritative server returns IP address for www.example.com
Resolver caches result and returns to client

questions.question: What is DNSSEC and why is it important for DNS security?
keyPoints: DNSSEC stands for DNS Security Extensions
Adds cryptographic signatures to DNS records
Verifies authenticity of DNS data
Prevents DNS spoofing and cache poisoning attacks
Uses public-key cryptography for verification

questions.question: Explain the concept of TTL in DNS and its impact on DNS performance.
keyPoints: TTL (Time To Live) specifies how long DNS records should be cached
Measured in seconds, common values: 300 to 86400
Lower TTL: More frequent queries, better accuracy during changes
Higher TTL: Reduced query load, faster resolution from cache
Trade-off between performance and propagation speed

questions.question: Explain the hierarchical structure of DNS servers. Describe the roles of Root Name Servers, TLD Name Servers, and Authoritative Name Servers in DNS resolution.
keyPoints: Root Name Servers: Top of hierarchy, handle '.' (root) zone, direct queries to TLD servers
TLD Name Servers: Manage top-level domains (.com, .org, .edu), direct to authoritative servers
Authoritative Name Servers: Hold actual DNS records for domains, provide final answers
Resolution flow: Client → Recursive Resolver → Root → TLD → Authoritative → Response
Caching occurs at multiple levels to improve performance
This hierarchical design provides scalability and fault tolerance

questions.question: Discuss the various types of DNS attacks and the security mechanisms available to protect DNS infrastructure.
keyPoints: DNS Cache Poisoning: Attacker injects false records into DNS cache
DNS Spoofing: Forging DNS responses to redirect traffic
DNS Tunneling: Exfiltrating data through DNS queries
DDoS attacks on DNS: Flooding servers to make them unavailable
DNSSEC: Cryptographic signatures to verify authenticity
DNS over HTTPS (DoH): Encrypts DNS queries
Rate limiting and query validation
Anycast routing for redundancy
